title: AgentForge Merge Guard
summary: A GitHub-first change-control system that makes high-risk and agent-assisted pull requests deterministic, reviewable, and auditable.
challenge: Teams adopting AI-assisted development need stronger evidence and decision trails around risky changes. Detection alone is insufficient: governance must identify the applicable policy, required evidence, accountable reviewer, decision state, and policy version without delegating blocking authority to a probabilistic model.
architecture_intro: A signed GitHub event enters a queue-backed evaluation pipeline. Deterministic packages derive facts, apply versioned policy, persist the decision record, and publish the result back to the pull request.

architecture.label: Ingest
architecture.detail: Fastify verifies GitHub webhook signatures and records deliveries before evaluation.

architecture.label: Queue
architecture.detail: BullMQ separates webhook acknowledgement from asynchronous pull-request evaluation.

architecture.label: Evaluate
architecture.detail: Detectors extract verified facts; policy-as-code resolves evidence and reviewer requirements.

architecture.label: Record
architecture.detail: PostgreSQL stores policy versions, evaluations, audit events, and Change Control Records.

architecture.label: Decide
architecture.detail: GitHub checks expose pass, warning, block, or authorized override outcomes to humans.

ownership: Defined the product boundary: deterministic governance rather than autonomous code approval.
Designed the TypeScript monorepo across API, dashboard, worker, policy, detector, evidence, reviewer, record, and security packages.
Established gradual adoption modes—observe, warn, enforce, and optimize—so policy can mature without an abrupt rollout.
Documented deployment, self-hosting, policy tuning, testing, security handling, and operational recovery paths.

decisions.title: Deterministic authority
decisions.detail: Blocking outcomes come from explicit rules and verified facts. Optional AI output is disabled by default and advisory only.

decisions.title: Durable change records
decisions.detail: Each governed pull request produces an authoritative Change Control Record tied to the policy version, evidence, reviewers, checks, and overrides.

decisions.title: Metadata-first storage
decisions.detail: The system excludes source-code storage by default, redacts secrets, and keeps exported records focused on governance evidence.

decisions.title: Fail-closed production
decisions.detail: Signed webhooks, trusted actor identity, session controls, and storage policy are explicit readiness requirements rather than permissive fallbacks.

controls: Signed GitHub webhook verification and duplicate-delivery handling
Policy schema validation, applicability scoping, and versioned policy packs
Role-constrained overrides with required reason, visibility, and audit trail
Production readiness checks for database, queue, authentication, and proxy trust
Human approval retained for risk acceptance
validation: Unit coverage for policy parsing, detectors, evidence, reviewer routing, redaction, overrides, and records
Integration coverage for webhook ingestion, policy preview, evidence updates, overrides, and exports
Fixture scenarios covering sensitive paths, CI changes, test weakening, dependencies, migrations, secrets, and overrides
Playwright end-to-end coverage for dashboard rendering and primary workflows
Performance sanity checks for large changed-file lists and repeated synchronize events
outcomes: A working self-hosted V1 spanning API, dashboard, asynchronous worker, PostgreSQL persistence, and GitHub check publication.
An explainable decision trail that answers what triggered, what is missing, who must review, and which policy version applied.
A controlled adoption path from visibility-only observation to enforceable merge protection.
A reusable governance model: deterministic checks decide, AI explains, and humans approve risk.
limitations: Merge Guard reports whether configured requirements are satisfied; it does not certify that code is secure, correct, or compliant.
The current release is self-hosted rather than a managed SaaS offering.
AI-based blocking decisions and autonomous merge approval are intentionally outside the product boundary.
Production object-storage export adapters remain outside the current V1 scope.
next_steps: Validate positioning and policy scope with external platform and security teams.
Expand deployment evidence and operational telemetry for enforce-mode adoption.
Evolve enterprise packaging only after user evidence supports retention, SSO, and audit-export needs.

title: Agentic Control Plane
summary: A bounded execution platform for goals, commitments, approvals, automations, memories, documents, and integrations.
challenge: Long-running agent work crosses authentication, integrations, background execution, approvals, recovery, and auditability. A useful control plane must keep these concerns connected while preventing an autonomous workflow from silently escalating into a high-risk external action.
architecture_intro: Requests and integration signals enter a governed API boundary, are classified for readiness and risk, and become durable jobs. The worker executes bounded work while the dashboard exposes commitments, approvals, evidence, recovery state, and learned context.

architecture.label: Capture
architecture.detail: The Next.js API accepts goals, commitments, watcher events, and integration signals with actor context.

architecture.label: Govern
architecture.detail: Policy and connector readiness classify risk, permissions, preconditions, and approval requirements.

architecture.label: Enqueue
architecture.detail: Long-running activity becomes a durable worker job instead of remaining inside a request lifecycle.

architecture.label: Execute
architecture.detail: A separate worker processes bounded tasks with concurrency controls, idempotency, and recovery paths.

architecture.label: Evidence
architecture.detail: PostgreSQL-backed state records artifacts, audit events, approvals, provenance, and learned context.

ownership: Designed the commitment-first product model and the bounded execution loop connecting dashboard, APIs, worker, and persistence.
Implemented governance boundaries for actor context, workspace roles, approvals, mutation preconditions, rate limits, and external side effects.
Structured integrations and preview capabilities around explicit readiness signals instead of presenting incomplete surfaces as production-ready.
Created operational runbooks for worker durability, recovery, observability, connector reconciliation, security response, and deployment.

decisions.title: Separate request from execution
decisions.detail: Long-running work is queued for a dedicated worker, keeping API lifecycles bounded and making retries and recovery explicit.

decisions.title: Approval before consequence
decisions.detail: Higher-risk external actions require an approval boundary; connectors do not gain implicit authority from an agent request.

decisions.title: Capability honesty
decisions.detail: Feature readiness is tracked in code so preview integrations and incomplete workflows are not represented as fully operational.

decisions.title: Durable operating context
decisions.detail: Goals, commitments, artifacts, audit state, and memories are persisted as product state rather than hidden inside a transient model conversation.

controls: Authenticated API routes with actor and workspace context
Role permissions, mutation preconditions, request identity, and abuse-rate controls
Approval gates for higher-risk external side effects
Idempotency and durable job execution for retry-safe workflows
Security headers, CSP, public-origin validation, and controlled sharing
Operational readiness, recovery, and connector reconciliation surfaces
validation: Repository-level type, lint, unit, integration, and smoke gates across the monorepo
Security regression coverage for APIs, authentication, side effects, sharing, and connector boundaries
Capability smoke checks that fail when production claims lack required readiness evidence
Runbook-backed validation for worker durability, connector recovery, observability, and deployment
Audit matrices connecting controls to implementation and executable evidence
outcomes: A modular TypeScript control plane with a Next.js dashboard/API, separate worker, and PostgreSQL production state.
A coherent execution model connecting commitments, approvals, automations, documents, memories, integrations, and recovery.
Explicit product readiness boundaries that separate operational core workflows from preview capabilities.
A reusable governance approach for keeping human accountability inside autonomous and human-in-the-loop work.
limitations: Several integration, watcher, memory, workflow-template, and autopilot surfaces remain preview capabilities until their readiness gates are satisfied.
Production operation depends on correctly configured shared state, authentication, workers, and connector credentials.
The control plane coordinates work; it cannot remove the underlying reliability and authorization constraints of external providers.
Higher autonomy remains intentionally bounded by governance and approval requirements.
next_steps: Close capability blockers before promoting preview surfaces to operational status.
Strengthen end-to-end recovery evidence for connector and worker failure modes.
Continue reducing operator load through calibrated batching, recommendations, and traceability.

title: Apple Calendar MCP Server
summary: A native Swift bridge that gives AI assistants and command-line workflows controlled access to Apple Calendar without sending calendar data to a hosted intermediary.
challenge: Calendar automation is useful but sensitive: the system must cross macOS privacy permissions, support both human and agent workflows, and prevent a convenient write API from becoming unrestricted authority over every calendar.
architecture_intro: A single Swift executable supports MCP, CLI, and native app modes. Each mode shares typed command models and one calendar-service boundary, which applies macOS permission, read-only policy, and calendar allowlist checks before EventKit writes.

architecture.label: Interface
architecture.detail: MCP JSON-RPC, CLI commands, and the native app translate user intent into typed requests.

architecture.label: Validate
architecture.detail: Strict argument parsing rejects unknown fields, invalid dates, unsupported URLs, and excessive search ranges.

architecture.label: Authorize
architecture.detail: Server configuration enforces read-only mode and an optional exact calendar-ID write allowlist.

architecture.label: Execute
architecture.detail: A shared EventKit service lists calendars, searches events, and performs allowed create, update, or delete operations.

architecture.label: Return
architecture.detail: Structured MCP responses and JSON or table CLI output make results useful to both programs and people.

ownership: Designed one Swift package to serve MCP clients, shell users, and a native macOS operator interface.
Implemented EventKit access, permission handling, typed request models, strict CLI parsing, MCP tool schemas, and structured output.
Added write controls that can block all mutations or restrict them to explicitly allowlisted calendar identifiers.
Built configuration guidance and app-generated MCP client settings so the security posture is visible and reproducible.

decisions.title: Local-first boundary
decisions.detail: Calendar access stays inside the macOS process and EventKit permission model; no hosted calendar proxy is required.

decisions.title: One service, three interfaces
decisions.detail: MCP, CLI, and app workflows share the same typed domain and authorization boundary instead of implementing divergent write paths.

decisions.title: Write scope is configuration
decisions.detail: Read-only mode and exact calendar-ID allowlisting turn mutation authority into an explicit operator decision.

decisions.title: Structured failure
decisions.detail: Permission, validation, calendar, event, and policy failures are modeled and returned instead of being flattened into ambiguous command errors.

controls: Native macOS Calendar permission through EventKit
Environment-controlled read-only mode for blocking create, update, and delete
Optional exact calendar-ID allowlist for write operations
Unknown-field rejection and typed request validation
366-day maximum search range and timezone-aware date validation
HTTP or HTTPS restriction for event URL values
validation: Swift package tests covering models, CLI parsing, MCP request handling, configuration, and calendar-service behavior
Read-only and write-allowlist policy tests around create, update, and delete operations
CLI output checks for human-readable tables and JSON automation output
Live permission-aware validation against Apple Calendar on macOS
Build and release checks for the executable and native app surfaces
outcomes: One local tool supports calendar listing, event search, creation, updates, and deletion over MCP and the command line.
Operators can inspect and change the write policy from the native app and copy a ready-to-use MCP configuration.
Automation can be constrained to read-only operation or a specific subset of writable calendars.
The design preserves the system macOS permission prompt and keeps calendar data out of a hosted middleware layer.
limitations: The tool is macOS-specific because it relies on EventKit and Apple Calendar permissions.
The operating system permission grant is shared by the server and native app process boundary.
Calendar identifiers and provider behavior remain subject to EventKit and the configured accounts on the local Mac.
Safe writes still depend on the operator choosing an appropriate read-only or allowlist configuration.
next_steps: Broaden live regression coverage across calendar account types and recurring-event operations.
Improve operator-facing diagnostics for permission and configuration mismatches.
Keep release packaging and client-configuration guidance aligned as MCP hosts evolve.
